back_translation: Agrippa spoke to Paul, saying, 'It is permitted for you to speak on your own behalf.' Then Paul stretched out his hand and made his defense, saying:
'O King Agrippa, regarding all the matters in which I am accused by the Jews, I consider myself to be highly fortunate that I will get to make my defense before your royal presence today,

especially because Your Majesty is fully acquainted with all the customs and disputed questions of the Jews. Therefore, I humbly entreat that Your Majesty would listen to me with a patient royal heart.
Therefore, my manner of life from young manhood, which has been from the beginning among my people and in Jerusalem, all Jews know well.
They have known me a long time, if they are willing to be witnesses, that I lived as a Pharisee, according to the strictest sect in our religion.
And now I stand being judged because of hope in the promise that God royally bestowed to our ancestors,

the twelve tribes of our people each hope to receive this promise fulfilled, while they serve God with intense earnestness night and day. O King, I am being accused by the Jews precisely because of this hope.
Why do all of you consider it an unbelievable matter that God should royally raise the dead?
In truth, even I myself once believed that I ought to take action against the divine name of Jesus the Nazarene to the utmost.

And that is what I did in Jerusalem — I confined many of the holy ones in prison, by means of authority received from the chief priests; and when they were punished to death, I cast my vote in agreement with that.
I often punished them frequently in every synagogue, and tried to pressure them to blaspheme. With extreme rage against them, I even pursued them to persecute them as far as cities of foreign lands.
While I was traveling to the city of Damascus on this matter, with authority and orders from the chief priests,
O King, around midday, on the road, I saw a light from the heavens, more dazzling than the sun, shining and surrounding me and those who were traveling with me.
When all of us had fallen to the ground, I heard a voice speaking to me in the Hebrew language, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me? It is hard for you to kick the goad.'
I therefore reverently asked, 'Who are you, master?' The Lord royally replied, 'I am Jesus, the one whom you are persecuting.

But arise and stand on your feet. For this reason I have appeared to you, in order to appoint you to be a servant and a witness, both of the things you have seen of me, and of the things I will reveal to you.
I will deliver you from your people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am sending you,

to open their eyes, that they may turn back from darkness to light, and from the authority of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins, and an inheritance among those whom He royally sanctifies through faith in me.'
Therefore, O King Agrippa, I was not stubborn against the vision from heaven.

But I proclaimed first to the people of Damascus, and in Jerusalem, throughout the region of Judea, and to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn back to God, doing deeds befitting repentance.
For this reason the Jews seized me at the temple precincts and tried to kill me.
But by help from God, I therefore stand here to this day, to bear witness to both the small and the great. I have not said anything beyond what the prophets and Moses said would happen,
namely, that the Christ must royally suffer, and being the first to rise from the dead, He will royally proclaim light to our people and to the Gentiles.'
While Paul was speaking his defense thus, Festus shouted in a loud voice, 'You have lost your mind, Paul! Your great learning is causing you to become deranged.'
But Paul replied, 'I have not lost my mind, honorable Festus. I am speaking words of truth and full mindfulness rather.'

For the king royally knows about these matters, therefore I dare to speak to His Majesty without concealment. I believe there is no matter among these things that has escaped His Majesty's royal awareness, because this matter did not happen in a hidden place.
O King Agrippa, does Your Majesty royally believe the prophets? I know that Your Majesty royally believes.'
Agrippa therefore royally said to Paul, 'You will persuade us to be a Christian in such a short time?'
Paul replied, 'Whether short time or long, I therefore pray to God, that not only Your Majesty, but that everyone who is listening to me today, would become like me, except for these chains.'
Then the king, the governor, Bernice, and all those who were sitting together each rose.
When they went out from that place, they conversed among themselves, saying, 'This man has not done anything worthy of death or imprisonment at all.'
Agrippa royally said to Festus, 'This man could already have been released, if he had not appealed to Caesar.'
